title: Energy saving in a flat or apartment
description: How to cut energy bills in a UK flat: electric heating tactics, off-peak tariffs, condensation fixes and summer cooling, with July 2026 price cap rates.
blurb: Flats beat houses on heating costs, but electric heating, condensation and summer overheating need their own playbook.
lede: A flat is the most energy-efficient home shape in Britain. Neighbours above, below and beside you act as free insulation, there is less external wall per square metre of living space, and there is no draughty loft hatch. Yet plenty of flat dwellers pay more than friends in houses, because flats come with their own traps: all-electric heating at roughly three times the useful cost of gas, communal systems that sit outside the price cap, condensation in winter and stifling heat in summer. The fixes are different from the standard house advice, and most of them are cheap.
short_answer: Flats are cheaper to run than houses before you change a single habit: Ofgem's low-usage band, which covers most flats, works out around £1,335 a year at the July to September 2026 price cap, against £1,863 for a medium house. The savings that matter most in a flat are getting the right tariff and routine if you heat with electricity, controlling moisture, and cheap draughtproofing on the front door and windows. Big-ticket house measures like loft and cavity wall insulation are mostly the freeholder's call, not yours.

sections.h2: Why a flat starts £500 ahead of a house
sections.html:
A mid-floor flat shares most of its surfaces with other heated homes, so it leaks far less heat than a house of the same floor area. Ofgem builds this into its typical consumption values: the low band, which covers most flats and one-bedroom homes, assumes 1,800 kWh of electricity and 7,500 kWh of gas a year. A medium three-bedroom house is assumed to use 2,700 kWh and 11,500 kWh.

Priced at the current cap, the gap is roughly £500 a year before anyone touches a thermostat:

Ofgem usage bandElectricityGasStanding chargesTotal per year
Low (typical flat or 1-bed): 1,800 kWh electricity, 7,500 kWh gas£470£550£315£1,335
Medium (2-3 bed house): 2,700 kWh electricity, 11,500 kWh gas£705£843£315£1,863
High (4-5 bed house): 4,100 kWh electricity, 17,000 kWh gas£1,071£1,246£315£2,632

At 26.11p per kWh electricity, 7.33p per kWh gas, and standing charges of 57.19p and 29.04p per day. Ofgem price cap, July to September 2026, GB direct-debit average.

Position in the block matters, though. A top-floor flat loses heat through the roof much like a bungalow, and a ground-floor flat sits over an unheated void or car park, so both will run colder and dearer than the mid-floor sweet spot. The other catch is control: the loft, the cavity walls and the roof belong to the freeholder, so the classic big-payback insulation jobs are usually off the table. What is left is heating strategy, moisture control and small kit, and in a flat those go a surprisingly long way.

sections.h2: All-electric heating: the big flat cost trap
sections.html:
Many blocks have no gas supply at all, and this is where flat bills go wrong. At the cap, electricity costs 26.11p per kWh, while a modern gas boiler delivers useful heat at around 8p per kWh once you allow for boiler efficiency. Every unit of heat from a plug-in heater therefore costs roughly three times as much as the same warmth in a gas-heated home.

  • A 2 kW panel or fan heater run flat out costs about 52p an hour. An evening of that, every evening, is £70 a month.
  • The consolation prize: an all-electric flat pays no gas standing charge, which claws back about £106 a year against a dual-fuel home.

If you have storage heaters, the whole game is pairing them with an off-peak tariff such as Economy 7, so they charge overnight at a much lower unit rate and release that heat through the day. Dig out your bill and find your actual night rate; on many Economy 7 tariffs it is around half the standard unit price, which closes most of the gap with gas. If your heaters keep running out of warmth by evening, the input and output dials are usually set wrong rather than the heaters being broken; our guide to how storage heaters work walks through the controls.

Two habits to avoid. First, topping up with the convector or boost setting during peak daytime hours, which buys the most expensive heat in the building. Second, leaving electric heaters on low all day because someone told you it is cheaper; it is not, and it sits high on our list of energy saving myths. Heat the hours you are home, and let the fabric of the block do the rest.

sections.h2: On communal heating or a heat network?
sections.html:
Some blocks pipe heat from a shared plant room or a district energy centre into each flat through a heat interface unit. If that is your setup, your heating is not covered by the Ofgem price cap and you cannot switch supplier for it; the freeholder or management company buys energy in bulk and passes the cost on through a tariff or the service charge.

You still have levers. Use the room thermostat and radiator valves rather than opening windows, because on most networks you pay for every unit your flat draws. Ask the operator for a clear tariff breakdown showing the unit rate and any fixed charges, and compare your metered use year on year rather than against capped gas prices. Report obvious waste such as permanently hot corridors or uninsulated pipe runs, since network losses end up on residents' bills.

Heat networks have moved into formal regulation, with Ofgem taking on the regulator role and consumer protections being phased in. Check the current rules, pricing oversight and complaint routes on Ofgem's heat networks pages, as the details are still changing.

sections.h2: Quick wins that survive a tenancy agreement
sections.html:
Almost everything here costs under £20, needs no permission from a landlord or freeholder, and moves out with you.

  • Draughtproof the front door first. Flat front doors often open onto an unheated communal corridor or stairwell, which behaves like outdoors. Self-adhesive foam or brush strip around the frame plus a letterbox brush costs a few pounds and takes twenty minutes.
  • Tame the electric shower. An 8.5 kW shower costs about 37p per ten minutes at the cap. Cutting one daily shower from ten minutes to five saves around £67 a year per person, which makes a £3 shower timer the best-value gadget in the flat.
  • LEDs in every fitting. Small homes still rack up lighting hours, and swapping any leftover halogens pays back within months.
  • Kill standby properly. In a one-room living space the TV, console, router and soundbar cluster on one or two sockets, so a single switched extension lead deals with most of it.
  • Dress the windows. Thermal curtain liners and clear secondary-glazing film transform a single-glazed flat in winter, and both peel away at the end of a tenancy.

Our quick wins under a tenner guide ranks these by payback, and the winter energy checklist turns them into a one-afternoon job list before the cold sets in.

sections.h2: Condensation in winter, overheating in summer
sections.html:
Condensation and damp

A flat is a small, well-sealed volume, and a single load of washing dried on an airer releases around two litres of water into it. That moisture has to condense somewhere, usually the coldest corner of the coldest bedroom. The cheap defences: lids on pans, the extractor fan running during and after showers (a 20 W fan costs about half a penny per hour, so leave it on), internal doors shut while cooking or bathing, and a window cracked for ten minutes rather than left ajar all day. Wiping condensation off windows each morning removes water before it feeds mould. If black spots have already appeared, our cheap condensation and mould fixes guide covers treatment and prevention in order of cost.

Summer overheating

The same compact, well-insulated shape that keeps a flat cheap in January makes it roast in July, especially top-floor and south or west facing flats. Close blinds and curtains before the sun reaches the glass, not after the room has heated up. Ventilate hard at night and in the early morning when outside air is cooler, then shut everything by mid-morning. A 50 W fan run for eight hours costs about 10p; a typical 1 kW portable air conditioner costs about 26p an hour and needs a window open for its exhaust hose, which undoes part of its own work. Try the free measures for a week before spending £300 on a unit you will use for ten days a year.

A flat already has the cheapest shell in British housing, so stop worrying about the insulation you cannot install and work the levers you do control. If you are all-electric, the heating tariff and the storage heater routine are worth more than every other measure combined. If you are on a heat network, learn your tariff and use the controls. Everyone else: draughtproof the front door, shorten the electric shower, manage moisture in winter and sunlight in summer. None of it needs the freeholder's permission, and most of it costs less than a takeaway.

faq.q: Are flats cheaper to heat than houses?
faq.a: Usually, yes. A flat shares walls, floor or ceiling with heated neighbours, so it loses far less heat. On Ofgem's typical consumption values, a low-usage flat costs around £500 a year less than a medium house at the July to September 2026 price cap. Top-floor and ground-floor flats sit somewhere in between because they lose heat through the roof or floor.

faq.q: My flat is all-electric. Am I stuck with high bills?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Electricity costs roughly three times as much per unit of heat as gas, but flats need far fewer units, and an all-electric home pays no gas standing charge, saving about £106 a year. The key is using storage heaters with an off-peak tariff such as Economy 7 and avoiding plug-in heaters at peak daytime rates.

faq.q: Is communal heating covered by the energy price cap?
faq.a: No. Heat networks and communal systems sit outside the Ofgem price cap because the building operator buys the energy in bulk. Heat networks are now moving under Ofgem regulation with new consumer protections, so check Ofgem's heat networks pages for the current rules and complaint routes.

faq.q: What can I change in a rented flat without the landlord's permission?
faq.a: Plenty. Self-adhesive draughtproofing strip, a letterbox brush, secondary-glazing film, thermal curtain liners, LED bulbs, a switched extension lead for standby loads and a shower timer all need no permission and can move out with you. Anything screwed to the fabric of the building, like a fixed draught excluder or new extractor fan, needs the landlord's agreement.

faq.q: Why does my flat get so much condensation?
faq.a: A flat is a small, airtight volume, so everyday moisture from cooking, showers and indoor clothes drying has nowhere to go and condenses on the coldest surfaces. Running extractor fans during and after cooking and bathing, keeping lids on pans, shutting internal doors and ventilating in short bursts deals with most of it for pennies.
